title: Low back pain basics (what most guidelines agree on)
summary: Most low back pain episodes improve over time. Key strategies include education, staying active, and an exercise program that you can stick with.
detail_md:
## Key idea
Low back pain is extremely common. Most cases are **non-specific** (no single identifiable tissue source) and often improve.

## What helps most
- **Advice & education** tailored to you
- **Continue normal activities** as tolerated (avoid prolonged bed rest)
- **Exercise** (general, motor control, strengthening, aerobic, or mind–body) chosen to fit you

## In this app
You’ll follow a simple plan and track how your symptoms respond over 24 hours to guide progression.

title: Stay active (the goal is to keep moving safely)
summary: Guidelines consistently encourage staying active and returning to normal activities as soon as possible, with symptom-limited pacing.
detail_md:
## Why it matters
Avoiding all activity can reduce confidence and decondition muscles. Most guidance encourages **continuing normal activities** and gradually rebuilding capacity.

## Practical rule
Choose activities you can do **without a big symptom spike** during, after, or the next day. If you flare, reduce and rebuild.

## Walking counts
Walking is often a safe starting point and can be progressed over time.

title: Do I need an MRI? (often: no, unless it changes care)
summary: Many guidelines advise against routine imaging for low back pain unless there are red flags or results would change management.
detail_md:
## Imaging basics
Imaging can show age-related changes even in people without pain. That can be confusing.

## Guideline principle
Imaging is generally considered in specialist settings **only if results are likely to change management**.

## In this app
If you report red flags, we stop the plan and encourage urgent assessment.

title: Exercise therapy helps (but the “best” exercise is the one you’ll do)
summary: Evidence suggests exercise can reduce pain and improve function in chronic low back pain. Different exercise types can work; consistency matters.
detail_md:
## Evidence summary
Systematic reviews suggest **exercise can reduce pain and improve disability** compared with minimal care, though effects vary and certainty is not perfect.

## What counts as exercise
- Walking/aerobic
- Strengthening
- Motor control/stabilisation
- Mind–body (yoga/tai chi)

## The app strategy
Start small, pick a plan you can adhere to, and progress conservatively.

title: Motor control exercises (what are they?)
summary: Motor control exercises train the timing and endurance of trunk muscles to support comfortable movement and confidence.
detail_md:
## Examples
Dead bug, bird dog, side plank variations.

## Why they’re used
Many rehab approaches include trunk coordination/stabilisation exercises as part of an overall exercise program.

## In this app
We start with easy variations and only progress when your 24-hour response is stable.

title: Pain monitoring (how we decide to progress)
summary: Your pain response over 24 hours is the key signal. The goal is gradual progress without big flare-ups.
detail_md:
## The 24-hour check
After a session, you’ll log pain, and the next day you’ll be prompted to log next-day symptoms.

## Default threshold
This app defaults to a conservative threshold (e.g., **≤3/10** during or after exercise *and* back to baseline the next day). You can personalise this (Premium) with warnings.

## Important
Severe or worsening neurological symptoms are not normal exercise soreness—use the safety screen.

title: Walking to reduce recurrence (simple, scalable)
summary: A large randomized trial found an individualized walking + education program reduced recurrence risk of activity-limiting low back pain.
detail_md:
## What the trial tested
A progressive walking plan + education delivered over months.

## What this app does
We provide a simplified progressive walking target and track flare patterns.

## Safety
Progress gradually; if symptoms spike, scale back.

title: Red flags: when to seek urgent care
summary: Certain symptoms require urgent medical assessment. Apps are not appropriate for emergencies.
detail_md:
## Seek urgent care if you have
- New bowel or bladder control problems
- Numbness in the saddle area (inner thighs/genitals)
- Severe or progressive weakness, or legs giving way
- Fever with severe back pain
- Severe night pain or unexplained weight loss
- Major trauma or suspected fracture

## In this app
If you report these, we show **Stop & Seek Care** and pause the plan.
